titleStmt.title: Letter from James Davidson to Clara Davidson, 12 April 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: James Davidson

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from James Davidson (1877-1916) to his mother, Clara Davidson, informing her that he has finally reached his destination. Davidson writes about the long journey and the new living accommodations, which were increasingly uncomfortable as troops were crushed in everywhere. Davidson takes these new living arrangements as a sign that they are no doubt planning for something big which will require packing in as many soldiers as quickly as possible.This letter is part of a large correspondence from Captain James Davidson to his parents. Davidson, who was Managing Director of the Sirocco Engineering Works in Belfast, served with 13 Royal Irish Rifles and the Machine Gun Corps. His letters describe his experiences while training in England and serving in France. He was killed on 1 July 1916, the first day of the Battle of the Somme.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
12/4/16 My dearest Mother - I arrived at my
destination very late last night after
30 hours of continuous travelling.
The journey seems much worse on
the way back when one has nothing
pleasant to look forward to. We were
fortunate in getting a good crossing
but the train journey - 16 hours was
awfully tiresome. On arrival at
railhead I found out that the Coy
had been relieved & was back in
reserve again so had not so far
to go to join it. The billets however
are by no means as comfortable
as where we were - troops are
being crushed in everywhere &
although there is not the responsibility
of holding a part of the line I would
much prefer being where we were.
I dare say from now on we shall
Have to content ourselves with any
old place for there is no doubt we
are preparing for something big &
it will be a case of packing as many
troops as possible into the area.
Have not yet heard how long we
are to be in reserve but don't
suppose it will be for more than
a week or so. Have had a
long & busy day of it with inspections
& c& then Dermot Hill goes on leave
tomorrow morning which meant going
over no end of papers with him so
excuse this short note. Weather
awful - rain & storm & so cold -
it makes coming back feel all
the worse. With fondest
love to all
Your loving son Jim
On Active Service
recd.17th April /16 Dated on receipt by Army Postmaster?
by Original hand Mrs. S.C. Davidson Seacourt Bangor Co. Down Ireland Signature of sender in bottom left corner J Davidson Date sent along bottom of envelope 12/4/16>
